en:
The Cutty Sark was one of the fastest sailing clippers ever built.
For years she carried tea from China before steam ships took over the trade.
Today she rests at Greenwich, where visitors can walk her decks and feel her majesty.
Workers had been restoring the old vessel for months when a sudden blaze broke out.
By the time the fire was brought under control, much of the upper deck had been destroyed.
Fortunately the keel and many original timbers survived.
A long programme of repair followed, and the ship has been open to the public ever since.
She still stands as a symbol of an era when speed under sail meant everything.
cn:
「卡蒂萨克」号是史上最快的帆船之一。
多年来她从中国运茶，直到蒸汽船取代帆船贸易。
如今她停泊在格林威治，游客可以走上甲板感受其雄伟。
工人们修缮这艘老船好几个月后，突发大火。
火势被控制住时，上层甲板大半已被烧毁。
幸好龙骨和许多原始木料幸存下来。
随后进行了长期修复，船只一直对公众开放。
她仍是那个时代——帆下速度即一切——的象征。

en:
Collectors of antique biscuit tins will pay large sums for rare designs.
One day an old man read an advertisement in the paper asking for a very large tin.
He realized that the tin in his kitchen had been issued in limited numbers years ago.
Several collectors visited him, and each offer was higher than the last.
He told them that the tin had been kept in his family since his childhood.
In the end he decided not to sell it at any price.
Instead, it was donated to a local museum, where it has been displayed ever since.
The old man said he preferred sharing it with everyone.
cn:
古董饼干筒收藏家愿意为稀有图案出高价。
一天，一位老人在报上看到征购一只特大饼干筒的广告。
他意识到厨房里的那只筒是多年前限量发行的。
好几位收藏家登门，一次出价高过一次。
他告诉他们，这只筒从小就在家里留着。
最终他决定无论出价多少都不卖。
相反，它捐给了当地博物馆，此后一直展出。
老人说他更愿意与大家分享。

en:
A pedlar travelled to a remote village hoping to sell his stock of goods.
When he arrived, he found a community that was almost completely self-sufficient.
The villagers bartered grain and cloth among themselves and used no money at all.
He had nothing they wanted to buy, and they had nothing he could sell.
At first he felt puzzled by this isolated way of life.
Later he traded a pair of scissors for a bag of potatoes and ate with the villagers.
He returned home with an empty cart but felt strangely content.
It was a place with nothing to sell and nothing to buy, yet full of quiet dignity.
cn:
一个小贩跋涉到偏远山村，想卖掉存货。
到达后他发现，这里的社群几乎完全自给自足。
村民以粮换布，根本不用钱。
他无货可售，他们也无物可买。
起初他对这种与世隔绝的生活感到困惑。
后来他用一把剪刀换了一袋土豆，与村民共餐。
他空车而归，却感到一种奇妙的满足。
那是个无货可售也无物可买、却充满宁静尊严的地方。

en:
A pearl merchant was walking on the beach when a fisherman showed him a huge pearl.
The man asked what he would pay, hoping for a fortune.
After careful examination, the merchant offered five pounds.
The fisherman felt the offer was an insult and refused to sell.
They argued until the merchant insisted the pearl had a flaw and was not worth more.
In a rage the fisherman tossed the pearl back into the sea.
Only later did the merchant realize what he had lost.
The pearl had been precious enough to make them both rich, but pride had ruined the chance.
cn:
一位珍珠商在海边散步，渔夫给他看一颗巨珠。
渔夫问肯出多少钱，指望发笔横财。
商人仔细察看后出价五镑。
渔夫觉得受辱，不肯卖。
争执中商人坚持珍珠有瑕疵，不值更多。
渔夫一怒把珍珠扔回海里。
商人事后才意识到自己失去了什么。
那颗珍珠本足以让两人都发财，骄傲却毁了机会。

en:
Television comedy shows depend on making audiences laugh, but humour is highly subjective.
What one generation finds funny, another may find dull or even offensive.
Telling jokes well is harder than it looks on screen.
It is not easy to explain why a single line makes one person laugh and another frown.
Producers try every format, from live comics to canned laughter, yet success is never guaranteed.
Understanding British humour, in particular, takes time for outsiders.
That a joke is funny to some and not to others is clear to every comedian.
In the end, whether something is funny or not depends on who is watching.
cn:
电视喜剧靠逗笑观众，但幽默高度主观。
一代人觉得好笑的，另一代人可能觉得无聊甚至冒犯。
把笑话讲好笑，比屏幕上看起来难得多。
很难解释为什么同一句话有人捧腹有人皱眉。
制作人尝试各种形式，从现场喜剧到预录笑声，成功却从未有保障。
尤其是理解英式幽默，外人需要时间。
笑话对有些人好笑、对有些人不好笑，每个喜剧演员都清楚。
归根结底，是否可笑取决于谁在观看。

en:
For years the old house was said to be haunted by a ghost.
Doors opened and closed at night, and footsteps were heard in empty rooms.
When a new tenant moved in, he decided to investigate the rumours.
He searched the attic and discovered a hidden room behind a panel.
Inside he found an old servant who had been living there in secret for years.
The man had once worked in the house and had never left.
He was taken to hospital, where he died shortly afterwards.
The townspeople joked that the ghost was dead at last, and the haunting was over.
cn:
多年来这座老宅据说闹鬼。
夜里门自行开合，空房间传来脚步声。
新租客搬入后决定调查传闻。
他搜查阁楼，在镶板后发现一间密室。
里面是一个藏身多年的老仆人。
他曾在宅中帮佣，此后再未离开。
他被送去医院，不久去世。
镇上人开玩笑说幽灵终于「死」了，闹鬼就此终结。

en:
Lord Ligonier was one of the most lovable eccentrics in the neighbourhood.
He insisted on wearing Victorian clothes when he went to buy vegetables.
He carried an umbrella instead of a walking stick and gave gold coins to strangers.
Every Christmas he would drive an old carriage through the streets and throw gifts to children.
People said his habits were odd, but no one disliked him.
He was generous to everyone and took pride in surprising people.
The whole neighbourhood knew his strange routines and looked forward to them.
He proved that eccentricity, if kind, can be a gift rather than a fault.
cn:
莱尼涅勋爵是街坊中最可爱的怪人之一。
他去买菜也坚持穿维多利亚式服装。
他撑伞而不拄拐，还把金币送给陌生人。
每年圣诞节他都会驾旧马车穿街过巷，向孩子抛礼物。
人们说他习惯古怪，却没人讨厌他。
他对每个人都慷慨，以出人意料为乐。
整条街都熟悉他古怪的例行举动，并有所期待。
他证明怪癖若出于善意，可以是天赋而非缺点。

en:
The Revenge was a sailing ship loaded with treasure when she sank in a storm centuries ago.
For generations explorers had searched for the wreck without success.
Ancient records described the cargo but not the exact position on the seabed.
A modern team used underwater equipment to scan the area near the coast.
At last they located the wreck lying in deep water.
Most of the treasure had been corroded by salt and time.
The ship herself, however, was of great archaeological value.
The discovery proved that a lost ship could still tell a story after hundreds of years.
cn:
「复仇号」在数世纪前满载财宝沉没于风暴中。
一代代探险者搜寻沉船，均告失败。
古籍记载了货物，却没有海底确切位置。
现代探险队用海底设备扫描近海区域。
他们终于在深水中定位了沉船。
大部分财宝已被盐水和岁月腐蚀。
船体本身却具有重大考古价值。
这一发现证明，一艘失落多年的船仍能讲述故事。

en:
It was a morning I shall never forget.
The sun was shining and I was driving to work, listening to the radio.
At a busy junction a truck suddenly ran out in front of me.
I swerved hard, mounted the pavement, and skidded back onto the road.
The car ended up at an angle by the kerb, but no one was seriously hurt.
While I was still shaking, the police and an ambulance arrived.
It was miraculous that a collision had been avoided.
Only later did the shock of those few seconds fully hit me.
cn:
那是一个我永生难忘的早晨。
阳光灿烂，我开车上班，听着收音机。
在繁忙路口，一辆卡车突然冲到我面前。
我急打方向盘，冲上人行道，又滑回路面。
车歪在路边，所幸无人重伤。
我还在发抖时，警察和救护车赶到了。
没撞上真是奇迹。
直到事后，那几秒钟的震惊才完全袭来。

en:
An antique dealer was passing a farmhouse when he noticed an old wooden chest.
The farmer was using it to store firewood and was happy to sell it for a few pounds.
Back at the shop, the dealer cleaned the chest and found a secret layer in the base.
Hidden inside was a small oil painting from the seventeenth century.
The panel had been concealed for over two hundred years.
The painting proved worth far more than the chest itself.
When the farmer heard the news, he was proud to have owned it, even unknowingly.
If he had known the truth, he would not have sold it so cheaply, he admitted with a laugh.
cn:
一位古董商路过农舍，注意到一只旧木箱。
农夫用它储柴，几镑就愿意卖。
回到店里清理时，商人在箱底发现夹层。
里面藏着一幅十七世纪的小型油画。
画板已被隐藏了两百多年。
画作价值远超木箱本身。
农夫得知后，为自己曾无意拥有它而自豪。
他笑着说，若早知道真相，就不会卖这么便宜了。

en:
A jeweller's shop was robbed and the thief was soon convicted in court.
The stolen diamonds, however, were never found by the police.
The owner investigated on his own and learned where the gems had been hidden.
They had been concealed in the thief's sister's flat across town.
One night he entered the flat and recovered the jewellery.
He left a note on the table: Justice was done.
The police suspected the truth but lacked enough evidence to act.
The owner felt that, in the end, justice had been done even without a second trial.
cn:
一家珠宝店遭窃，小偷很快在法庭定罪。
被盗钻石警方却始终未找到。
店主自行调查，得知宝石藏匿之处。
它们被藏在小偷妹妹位于城另一头的公寓里。
一天夜里他进入公寓，取回了珠宝。
他在桌上留了张字条：正义已伸张。
警方疑心真相，但证据不足，无法行动。
店主觉得，终究正义得以伸张，尽管没有第二次审判。

en:
She bought an old brooch for a few pounds in a second-hand shop because she liked the design.
She wore it occasionally and thought nothing more of it for years.
Then she took it to a television programme where experts identify antiques.
After careful study, they told her it was a royal diamond brooch that had been missing for decades.
It must have been lost or sold during troubled times long ago.
It can't have been a copy; the stones were real.
She could hardly believe her luck — a chance in a million.
The brooch was worth a fortune, yet she had paid almost nothing for it.
cn:
她在旧货店花几镑买了枚旧胸针，只因喜欢造型。
她偶尔佩戴，多年未多想。
后来她把胸针带到电视鉴宝节目。
专家仔细鉴定后告诉她，这是失踪数十年的王室钻石胸针。
它一定在多年前的动荡中被遗失或卖出。
不可能是仿品，宝石是真的。
她几乎不敢相信自己运气这么好——百万分之一的机遇。
胸针价值连城，她却几乎没花什么钱。
